name: async-python-patterns
description: Master Python asyncio, concurrent programming, and async/await patterns for high-performance applications. Use when building async APIs, concurrent systems, or I/O-bound applications requiring non-blocking operations.
capabilities: Guide selection of asyncio patterns (gather, tasks, queues, pools) based on workload
Implement concurrent I/O operations with proper timeout and error handling
Build async web APIs using FastAPI, aiohttp, or Sanic
Create async context managers and iterators for resource management
Debug and test async code paths with event loop inspection
Optimize I/O-bound workloads with structured concurrency
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is a documentation and educational resource for async Python programming patterns. The static analyzer flagged 71 potential issues, but upon manual review, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The 'external_commands' detections are markdown code fences (backticks), 'network' detections are example URLs in documentation, and 'blocker' detections are benign documentation patterns. No actual security risks were found.
valueStatement: Build high-performance Python applications with asyncio patterns for concurrent I/O operations, async APIs, and non-blocking systems.
limitations: Cannot execute code directly - provides guidance and examples only
Does not cover CPU-bound parallel processing (use multiprocessing instead)
Requires Python 3.7+ runtime with asyncio support
Cannot debug runtime issues without access to the actual code context

useCases.targetUser: Backend developers building performant web services
useCases.title: Build Async Web API
useCases.description: Create a high-performance REST API using FastAPI with concurrent database queries and async endpoints

useCases.title: Concurrent Data Fetching
useCases.description: Fetch multiple API resources simultaneously using asyncio.gather with proper error handling and timeouts

useCases.title: Real-time Application Backend
useCases.description: Implement WebSocket servers and async message handling for chat or live notification systems

promptTemplates.title: Basic Async Pattern Request
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to write a simple async function in Python that fetches data from a URL. Include proper error handling and a timeout.

promptTemplates.title: Concurrent Operations
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to fetch data from 5 different API endpoints concurrently. Show me how to use asyncio.gather with return_exceptions=True for error handling.

promptTemplates.title: Async Context Manager
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an async context manager that connects to a database, yields a connection, and properly closes it on exit. Include error handling.

promptTemplates.title: Production Async Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Build a production-ready async function that queries a database with connection pooling, implements retry logic with exponential backoff, includes structured logging, and handles cancellation gracefully.

bestPractices: Always add timeouts to prevent indefinite blocking - use asyncio.wait_for() or asyncio.timeout()
Use structured concurrency with asyncio.TaskGroup (Python 3.11+) for automatic cleanup
Handle exceptions explicitly with try/except inside async functions, never let exceptions silently propagate
antiPatterns: Do not use blocking synchronous calls inside async functions - this blocks the entire event loop
Avoid 'fire and forget' without proper task tracking - store Task references to enable cancellation
Do not mix await and blocking code like time.sleep() - use asyncio.sleep() instead

faq.question: When should I use asyncio vs threading?
faq.answer: Use asyncio for I/O-bound tasks (network, file, database) where waiting dominates. Use threading for CPU-bound tasks with true parallelism. Asyncio handles thousands of concurrent I/O operations with less memory than threads.

faq.question: How do I run sync blocking code in async context?
faq.answer: Use asyncio.to_thread() (Python 3.9+) or loop.run_in_executor() to run blocking sync code in a thread pool without blocking the event loop.

faq.question: What's the difference between gather, create_task, and wait?
faq.answer: asyncio.gather() runs all coroutines and returns all results together. create_task() schedules a single coroutine and returns a Task for later awaiting. asyncio.wait() returns done/pending sets without collecting results.

faq.question: How do I handle exceptions in concurrent tasks?
faq.answer: Use return_exceptions=True in gather() to catch all exceptions as results. For structured concurrency, use TaskGroup (Python 3.11+) which captures exceptions automatically and cancels other tasks on failure.

faq.question: Can I use async with outside of an async function?
faq.answer: No, async with must be inside an async function. Wrap it with asyncio.run() at the top level, or use a running event loop in Python 3.10+ with asyncio.current_task().

faq.question: How do I debug async code effectively?
faq.answer: Use PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG=1 environment variable for warnings. Use asyncio.get_running_loop().set_debug(True) for detailed stack traces. Install aiomonitor or use asyncio.sleep() breakpoints instead of regular breakpoints.
